name: Hamilton Depression Rating Scale
description: The most widely used clinician-administered depression assessment scale. The 17-item version assesses symptoms of depression experienced over the past week, with emphasis on melancholic and physical symptoms.
instructions: For each item, select the one cue which best characterizes the patient based on a structured clinical interview. Be sure to record the answers in the appropriate spaces.

options.label: These feeling states indicated only on questioning
These feeling states spontaneously reported verbally
Communicates feeling states non-verbally (facial expression, posture, voice, tendency to weep)
Patient reports virtually only these feeling states in spontaneous verbal and non-verbal communication
Self reproach, feels he/she has let people down
Ideas of guilt or rumination over past errors or sinful deeds
Present illness is a punishment. Delusions of guilt
Hears accusatory or denunciatory voices and/or experiences threatening visual hallucinations
Feels life is not worth living
Wishes he/she were dead or any thoughts of possible death to self
Attempts at suicide (any serious attempt rates 4)
Complains of occasional difficulty falling asleep (more than 1/2 hour)
Complains of nightly difficulty falling asleep
Patient complains of being restless and disturbed during the night
Waking during the night – any getting out of bed rates 2 (except for voiding)

questions.question: Insomnia: Early Hours of the Morning

options.label: Waking in early hours of the morning but goes back to sleep
Unable to fall asleep again if he/she gets out of bed
Thoughts and feelings of incapacity, fatigue or weakness related to activities, work or hobbies
Loss of interest in activity, hobbies or work – directly reported or indirect in listlessness, indecision, vacillation
Decrease in actual time spent in activities or decrease in productivity (less than 3 hours/day in activities)
Stopped working because of present illness. Engages in no activities except routine chores, or fails to perform routine chores unassisted

questions.question: Retardation (slowness of thought and speech, impaired ability to concentrate, decreased motor activity)

options.label: Hand wringing, nail biting, hair-pulling, biting of lips
Apprehensive attitude apparent in face or speech

questions.question: Anxiety Somatic (physiological concomitants: GI symptoms, cardiovascular, respiratory, urinary frequency, sweating)

options.label: Loss of appetite but eating without staff encouragement. Heavy feelings in abdomen
Difficulty eating without staff urging. Requests or requires laxatives or medication for bowels or GI symptoms
Heaviness in limbs, back or head. Backaches, headaches, muscle aches. Loss of energy and fatigability

questions.question: Genital Symptoms (loss of libido, menstrual disturbances)

options.label: Frequent complaints, requests for help, etc.

questions.question: Loss of Weight (rate according to patient report or weekly measurements)

options.label: No weight loss / Less than 1 lb weight loss in week
Probable weight loss associated with present illness / Greater than 1 lb weight loss in week
Definite (according to patient) weight loss / Greater than 2 lb weight loss in week
Acknowledges illness but attributes cause to bad food, climate, overwork, virus, need for rest, etc.

severityBands.label: Normal / Remission
severityBands.description: Within normal range or in clinical remission.

severityBands.label: Very Severe Depression
severityBands.description: Very severe depressive symptoms requiring urgent clinical attention.

interpretation.remissionCriteria: A score of 0-7 is generally accepted as within normal range or clinical remission.
interpretation.trialEntry: A score of 20 or higher (at least moderate severity) is usually required for entry into clinical trials.
interpretation.clinicalNotes: Item 3 (Suicide) should be carefully assessed as a risk indicator. The HDRS emphasizes melancholic and physical symptoms; atypical symptoms (hypersomnia, hyperphagia) are not assessed.
interpretation.riskItems: Item 3 (Suicide) - scores of 3 or 4 indicate significant suicide risk requiring immediate clinical attention.

references.citation: Hamilton M. A rating scale for depression. J Neurol Neurosurg Psychiatry. 1960;23:56-62.
Hamilton M. Development of a rating scale for primary depressive illness. Br J Soc Clin Psychol. 1967;6(4):278-96.
Williams JB. A structured interview guide for the Hamilton Depression Rating Scale. Arch Gen Psychiatry. 1988;45(8):742-7.
